topicArea: Curiosity, From Vice to Virtue & the Governance of the Itch

questions.passage:
The child has the radio in pieces across the carpet, and the question is what the adults will do about it. In one century, they see a small vice on display, an appetite for what is not the child's business, and confiscate the screwdriver. In another, they see a scientist in larval form and buy her a kit with more screws. The radio is the same radio; the impulse is the same impulse. What has changed is a long argument about whether wanting to know is a virtue, a vice, or something more unsettling than either, and the argument is worth reopening precisely because we now assume it is settled.

For a large stretch of Western moral thought, it was settled the other way. Curiosity stood condemned as a lust of the mind: the desire to know what does not concern you, directed at the neighbor's affairs, the forbidden art, the sealed room. The indictment was precise. Curiosity, the tradition held, is appetite indifferent to the worth of its object, wanting-to-know for wanting's sake, and its social form is gossip, its mythic form the opened box and the backward glance, its theological form the reach for the one fruit in the garden that was fenced. What made it a vice was not knowledge but appetite unranked: the curious mind grazes without asking what deserves to be known.

The rehabilitation, when it came, was thorough. Early modern inquiry re-badged the itch as the engine of discovery, and the collector's cabinet gave it furniture; within two centuries the vice had become the official virtue of an entire civilization, cultivated in schools, celebrated in obituaries, demanded in job interviews. Something real was gained in the reversal, and something was blurred. Wonder and curiosity had once been distinguishable: wonder rests in the marvel, content to stand before what exceeds it, while curiosity dismantles the marvel to see the gears, and a culture that keeps only the second acquires great power over radios at some cost to its capacity for standing still.

Modern accounts have given the itch an anatomy. Curiosity behaves like an information-gap emotion: it arouses not in total ignorance, where there is nothing to hook it, nor in mastery, where there is nothing left, but in the fertile middle where one knows enough to know exactly what one is missing. Hence the trivia question that torments until answered, the cliffhanger, the sealed envelope. Hence also the manipulability: a gap can be engineered, and whole industries now cut information into shapes designed to open loops that only clicking closes. And hence the old indictment's stubborn residue, for the itch is famously indifferent to worth, tugging with equal force toward the enzyme's structure and the neighbor's divorce.

Which is why the ethics did not dissolve with the rehabilitation; it relocated. Curiosity about things and curiosity about persons are not the same act: the radio cannot be wronged by being opened, but a person can, and the old charge of prying survives as the recognition that to be curious about someone, past what they offer, treats an interior as a puzzle owed a solution. Privacy is the fence this appetite tests daily. None of this argues for re-condemning the impulse; it argues that an appetite this strong and this indifferent requires aim, and that governance is not the enemy of curiosity but the condition of its decency.

The institutions charged with cultivating it, meanwhile, exhibit a durable paradox. Schooling praises curiosity in its mission statements and is structured to exhaust it in its classrooms, where questions come with answers already keyed, where the syllabus penalizes wandering, and where the measured rate of student question-asking falls year over year as schooling proceeds. The appetite survives best at the edges, in the margins of notebooks and the dismantled radios of home, which suggests that institutions are better at rewarding curiosity's products than at tolerating its process.

So the adults standing over the carpet hold a truer position than either century's: the impulse on the floor is neither vice nor virtue but a power, and like every power it takes its morality from its objects and its governance. The mark of a serious culture is not maximal curiosity, which is only appetite with good publicity, but aimed curiosity, the itch taught what deserves it. The radio, to its credit, cannot be wronged by being opened. The neighbor can, and the difference between them is the entire remaining question.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Curiosity is a powerful appetite indifferent to its objects' worth, so the live question after its rehabilitation is how to aim and govern it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The early modern rehabilitation of curiosity was a mistake, and the older tradition's condemnation of the lust of the mind deserves restoration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because schools measurably extinguish question-asking, formal education is incompatible with genuine curiosity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The information-gap account proves that curiosity is a mechanical reflex that industries can trigger at will, leaving no meaningful room for individual aim.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage tracks the vice-to-virtue reversal, retains the old indictment's stubborn residue, appetite unranked, indifferent to worth, relocates the ethics to the person-thing boundary, and concludes that the impulse is a power taking its morality from its objects and governance: the mark of a serious culture is aimed curiosity, not maximal curiosity.
explanations.B: This choice restores a verdict the passage explicitly declines. None of this argues for re-condemning the impulse, the fifth paragraph says; the tradition's residue is honored as recognition of prying's wrong, while the rehabilitation's gain is called real.
explanations.C: This choice hardens the sixth paragraph's paradox into an incompatibility claim. Schooling exhausts what its mission statements praise, and the appetite survives at the edges, but the passage's conclusion concerns aim and governance, not the abolition or impossibility of institutional cultivation.
explanations.D: This choice inflates manipulability into determinism. Gaps can be engineered and industries cut information into loop-opening shapes, but the conclusion assigns the itch to governance and teaching, the appetite taught what deserves it, which presupposes exactly the room for aim this choice denies.

questions.stem: The primary function of the sixth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: recommend that schools replace their keyed-answer question formats with open-ended, student-directed project work.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that curiosity cannot be taught and must instead be inherited or self-generated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: expose the paradox of institutions that praise curiosity while structured to exhaust it, rewarding products but not process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: explain why the child in the opening scene chose to dismantle the radio at home rather than at school.

explanations.A: This choice extracts a curriculum reform from a diagnosis. The paragraph observes the structure, questions keyed, wandering penalized, question rates falling, without prescribing any pedagogy; the passage's recommendations concern aim and governance, not lesson design.
explanations.B: This choice converts institutional failure into impossibility. The appetite survives best at the edges is an observation about where it persists under current structures, not a claim that teaching is futile; the conclusion's aimed curiosity, the itch taught what deserves it, presupposes teachability.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph names its subject a durable paradox: schooling praises curiosity in its mission statements and is structured to exhaust it in its classrooms, with question-asking falling year over year, and draws the diagnosis explicitly, institutions are better at rewarding curiosity's products than at tolerating its process.
explanations.D: This choice retrofits the opening scene with a motive the passage never supplies. The dismantled radios of home appear as where the appetite survives, an illustration of the edges; nothing narrates the child's choice of venue or contrasts her school behavior.

questions.stem: The contrast wonder rests in the marvel while curiosity dismantles it, in the third paragraph, most nearly conveys that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wonder is the emotion of the scientifically untrained, while curiosity belongs to experts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: curiosity is destructive of physical objects, while wonder preserves them intact for others.

choices.label: C
choices.text: wonder and curiosity are successive developmental stages, with every genuine episode of wonder eventually maturing into systematic investigation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: wonder can remain before what exceeds it without resolving it, while curiosity is driven to take the marvel apart.

explanations.A: This choice sorts the two attitudes by credential, a distinction the passage never draws. The contrast concerns the stance toward the marvel, resting versus dismantling, and nothing assigns wonder to laity or curiosity to expertise.
explanations.B: This choice literalizes dismantling into vandalism. The gears are the point, not the wreckage: curiosity's dismantling is investigative, the drive to see how the marvel works, and the cost the paragraph names is a lost capacity for standing still, not broken objects.
explanations.C: This choice converts a distinction into a developmental sequence. The paragraph's worry is precisely that the two are separable, a culture that keeps only the second, which would be incoherent if wonder always ripened into curiosity as a stage.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph defines the pair against each other: wonder rests in the marvel, content to stand before what exceeds it, while curiosity dismantles the marvel to see the gears, and the sentence completes the thought with the cost, great power over radios at some cost to its capacity for standing still. The contrast is between abiding and resolving.

questions.stem: According to the second paragraph, the older tradition's indictment of curiosity held that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: knowledge itself corrupts, so the ignorant are morally safer than the learned.

choices.label: B
choices.text: inquiry into the natural world was fully acceptable, while inquiry into other persons constituted the true vice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: curiosity was dangerous chiefly because it distracted laborers from productive work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the vice lay in appetite unranked, wanting-to-know indifferent to whether its object deserves knowing.

explanations.A: This choice indicts knowledge where the tradition indicted appetite. The paragraph is explicit: what made it a vice was not knowledge but appetite unranked; the sealed room and the fenced fruit mark objects placed off-limits, not learning condemned as such.
explanations.B: This choice imports the fifth paragraph's modern relocation into the old indictment. The person-thing boundary is where the ethics moved after rehabilitation; the older charge swept the neighbor's affairs and the forbidden art together, grazing without ranking either.
explanations.C: This choice invents an economic complaint the paragraph never registers. The indictment is moral and theological, gossip, the opened box, the backward glance, the fenced fruit, with productivity nowhere among its counts.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph states the charge with the precision it credits to the tradition: curiosity is appetite indifferent to the worth of its object, wanting-to-know for wanting's sake, and what made it a vice was appetite unranked, the curious mind grazing without asking what deserves to be known.

questions.stem: A news site rewrites its headlines so that each withholds the story's key fact, promising the answer behind a click. Based on the fourth paragraph, this practice is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a modern cabinet of curiosities, assembling scattered marvels to reawaken the jaded reader's capacity for wonder.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the engineering of information gaps, cutting content into shapes that open loops only clicking closes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an appeal to the fertile middle that fails because readers of headlines are in total ignorance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a legitimate exercise of the rehabilitated virtue, since it induces readers to seek knowledge.

explanations.A: This choice borrows the third paragraph's furniture for the wrong occupant. The cabinet housed the rehabilitated itch of inquiry; the withheld headline is the fourth paragraph's manufactured hook, and wonder, which rests rather than clicks, is precisely what the loop-opening design does not serve.
explanations.B: Correct. The fourth paragraph describes the practice in advance: a gap can be engineered, and whole industries now cut information into shapes designed to open loops that only clicking closes. The headline that names everything but the key fact constructs the fertile middle artificially, knowing enough to know exactly what one is missing, and sells the closure.
explanations.C: This choice misapplies the anatomy it cites. The withholding headline works because it supplies partial knowledge, the story's frame minus its key fact, which is the fertile middle manufactured, not total ignorance, where the paragraph says there is nothing to hook.
explanations.D: This choice launders manipulation through the rehabilitation. The fourth paragraph presents engineered gaps as exploitation of the itch's mechanics, the residue of the old indictment, indifference to worth, made profitable; inducing clicks is not the aimed curiosity the conclusion endorses.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the fourth paragraph's information-gap account of curiosity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Studies finding curiosity strongest under complete ignorance, declining steadily as knowledge accumulates, the middle zone showing no elevation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that some people report feeling curious about many topics simultaneously.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that television cliffhangers lose their grip entirely on those viewers who have already been told the episode's resolution well in advance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that most people regret the time they spend clicking engineered headlines.

explanations.A: Correct. The account's signature prediction is the inverted-U: arousal not in total ignorance, where there is nothing to hook it, nor in mastery, but in the fertile middle where one knows enough to know what one is missing. Curiosity peaking under complete ignorance and declining monotonically with knowledge would reverse that curve and strike the mechanism at its defining claim.
explanations.B: This choice reports breadth of interest, which the gap account accommodates without strain: a mind can hold many fertile middles at once, and simultaneous curiosity across topics says nothing about where, within each topic, the itch arises.
explanations.C: This choice confirms the account rather than testing it. A revealed resolution closes the loop, so the cliffhanger's lost grip is the gap mechanism behaving exactly as predicted when the gap is filled.
explanations.D: This choice measures regret about the manipulation's results, which presupposes the mechanism worked; remorse after clicking is evidence the engineered gap pulled as designed, not evidence against the anatomy.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the sixth paragraph's claim that schooling's structure, rather than students' aging, exhausts question-asking?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that teachers report wishing their students would ask more questions in class.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Studies finding question-asking steady with age among unschooled children while matched schooled peers declined, steepest where wandering penalties were strongest.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that university students ask noticeably fewer questions in large anonymous lecture halls than they do in small discussion-based seminars.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that most adults describe themselves as curious people despite their schooling.

explanations.A: This choice records teachers' wishes, which coexist comfortably with structures that suppress asking; the paragraph's paradox is precisely that institutions praise what their design exhausts, so goodwill among staff neither tests nor supports the structural claim.
explanations.B: Correct. The claim needs the decline attributed to structure rather than maturation. Steady rates among unschooled children of the same ages isolate schooling as the variable, and the decline steepening with penalty strength ties the exhaustion to the very design features the paragraph names, questions keyed and wandering penalized, a dose-response signature of structure at work.
explanations.C: This choice compares formats within schooling, lecture versus seminar, which shows structure modulating expression in the moment but cannot separate schooling's cumulative effect from aging, the confound the claim must escape.
explanations.D: This choice offers adult self-description, which the paragraph's own account predicts: the appetite survives best at the edges, so self-reported curiosity after schooling is compatible with the classroom exhaustion the claim describes, and measures identity rather than question-asking anyway.
